title: Effective data transmission through energy-efficient clustering and Fuzzy-Based IDS routing approach in WSNs

sections.name: Body
sections.content:
Virtual Reality & Intelligent Hardware
• Article • February 2024 V ol. 6 No. 1: 1–16
10.1016/j.vrih.2022.10.002
Effective data transmission through energy-efficient clustering and Fuzzy-Based IDS routing approach in WSNs
Saziya TABBASSUM1*, Rajesh Kumar PA THAK2
1. Research Scholar , Department of Computer Science, OPJS University Churu, Sadulpur , Rajasthan 331303, India;
2. Vice Chancellor , OPJS University Churu, Sadulpur , Rajasthan 331303, India
Received 23 June 2022; Revised 30 August 2022; Accepted 19 October 2022
Abstract: Wireless sensor networks (WSN) gather information and sense information samples in a certain region
and communicate these readings to a base station (BS). Energy efficiency is considered a major design issue in the
WSNs, and can be addressed using clustering and routing techniques. Information is sent from the source to the BS
via routing procedures. However, these routing protocols must ensure that packets are delivered securely, guaranteeing that neither adversaries nor unauthentic individuals have access to the sent information. Secure data
transfer is intended to protect the data from illegal access, damage, or disruption. Thus, in the proposed model,
secure data transmission is developed in an energy-effective manner. A low-energy adaptive clustering hierarchy
(LEACH) is developed to efficiently transfer the data. For the intrusion detection systems (IDS), Fuzzy logic and
artificial neural networks (ANNs) are proposed. Initially, the nodes were randomly placed in the network and
initialized to gather information. To ensure fair energy dissipation between the nodes, LEACH randomly chooses
cluster heads (CHs) and allocates this role to the various nodes based on a round-robin management mechanism. The
intrusion-detection procedure was then utilized to determine whether intruders were present in the network. Within
the WSN, a Fuzzy interference rule was utilized to distinguish the malicious nodes from legal nodes. Subsequently,
an ANN was employed to distinguish the harmful nodes from suspicious nodes. The effectiveness of the proposed
approach was validated using metrics that attained 97% accuracy, 97% specificity, and 97% sensitivity of 95%.
Thus, it was proved that the LEACH and Fuzzy-based IDS approaches are the best choices for securing data
transmission in an energy-efficient manner.
Keywords: Low energy adaptive clustering hierarchy (LEACH); Intrusion detection system (IDS); Wireless
sensor network (WSN); Fuzzy logic and artificial neural network (ANN)
Citation: Saziya TABBASSUM, Rajesh Kumar PA THAK. Effective data transmission through energy-efficient clustering and Fuzzy-Based IDS
routing approach in WSNs. Virtual Reality & Intelligent Hardware, 2024, 6(1): 1–16

sections.name: Introduction
sections.content:
Hundreds of thousands of sensor nodes are dispersed throughout the surroundings to sense, analyze, and
collect information in a wireless sensor network (WSN) [1]. Compared to the traditional sensor nodes, these
sensor nodes cost less and have superior sensing, processing, and transmission capabilities. They have wide
range of applications, such as in the weather forecasting, defense, medical applications, smart cities [2], and
Peer review under the responsibility of Beijing Zhongke Journal Publishing Co.Ltd.
2096-5796/©Copyright 2024 Beijing Zhongke Journal Publishing Co. Ltd., Publishing services by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of KeAi Communication Co. Ltd. This is
an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/).
*Corresponding author , saziyatabassum@gmail.com

many industrial and commercial areas
[3]
. WSN sensors are low-power consuming compact devices. The sensor
utilizes an analog-to-digital converter (ADC) to collect the data, which are analyzed before being forwarded to
the main site, referred to as the base station (BS). Before making decisions for various applications, the data
are analyzed at the BS. The WSN sensor nodes are repeaters that transmit data to other sensors and sinks. They
consume energ y in two ways: environmental parameter detection, and data transfer to the BS via nodes
[4]
.
Broadcasting data from the sensors to BSs and increasing the network lifetime require energ y-efficient data
transfer techniques. Clustering is a powerful approach that involves the grouping of sensor nodes to save
electricity
[5]
. The cluster member (CM) then detects the real-world environment and reports it to the cluster
head in-char ge (CH). Many researchers have previously presented numerous data dissemination protocols and
safe routing protocols with mobility modelling to improve the system using a remote method while considering the node-location problem. In a distributed network of nodes, data gathering and transmission to the
BS require several hops
[6]
.
Clustering and routing are two important WSN optimization problems. Numerous protocols have been
implemented, and the majority of clustering-related routing models have been evaluated. A wide range of
routing models linked to clustering have been introduced
[7]
. Computational intelligence (CI) techniques, such
as neural networks (NN)
[8]
, swarm intelligence (SI)
[9]
, and evolutionary algorithms (EA)
[10]
, have been used to
address the challenges in the WSN, such as privacy CH election, data aggregation, synchronization, and
routing. Owing to the sensitive data they handle and analyze, security is a topic of study that is gaining
increasing attention. A robust security solution is necessary to protect the WSN from numerous threats.
However , the number of WSN energy resources is often low . Consequently , diff erent security protocols cannot
be used directly to solve the diverse security problems.
The Intrusion Detection System (IDS), is largely responsible for operational security issues. It attempts to
protect and track abnormal network traf fic by distinguishing between normal and abnormal functionalities.
When an IDS reaches its maximum level, it is classified into two parts such as host IDS and network IDS.
Misallocation IDS, were originally referred to as signature-based IDS because they predicted intrusions by
watching events comparable to well-known attacks. The prediction of known and unknown attacks is less
successful. The detection of anomalies in an IDS generates a network behavior profile and detects abnormal
activity
[1 1]
. These attacks are unexpected and can cause network disruptions by compromising data availability .
A Distributed Denil of Service (DDoS) attack is easy to launch on a server; however , its eff ect is significant. A
cluster head placed near the BS depletes energ y at a faster rate.
Consequently , the network connection is interrupted, resulting in coverage problems called the hotspot
issues. Therefore, the WSNs require high efficiency and security to enable data transmission for more acceptable applications. The proposed technique uses a low-energy adaptive clustering hierarchy (LEACH) for
energ y-effective data transmission and Fuzzy-based IDS for secure data transmission in the WSN.
The following are the goals of the proposed method:
∙ Initially , the nodes are randomly located in the system, and information related to the neighbors is
collected.
∙ A LEACH is proposed for routing in homogeneous sensor networks to minimize energy consumption and
prolong network life.
∙ An intrusion detection system is used to detect the appearance of invaders. The IDS model operates in a
two-step isolation of legitimate nodes and detection of malicious attacks.
∙ A Fuzzy inference rule is suspected of being used to initially isolate the nodes from legitimate nodes within
a WSN.
∙ Subsequently , a trained neural network model is used to trace the malicious nodes from the suspected
nodes.
Virtual Reality & Intelligent Hardware February (2024) Vol. 6 No. 12

The rest of the paper is or ganized as follows: Section 2 presents the literature review related to data
transmission in WSN. Section 3 describes the proposed methodology and architecture of the proposed method.
The results and discussion are presented in Section 4. Finally , Section 5 concludes the study .
2 Literatur e r eview
Several methods have been used for energy-ef fective and secure data transmission in WSNs in an energ y
effective as well as secured manner . A few of these methods are described below .
Safaldin et al. suggested the new intrusion detection system (IDS) (GWOSVM-IDS) based on an altered
binary grey wolf optimizer and a support vector machine
[12]
. GWOSVM-IDS determined the appropriate
number of wolves using three, five, and seven wolves. The proposed technique in the WSN scenario focuses
on improving the intrusion detection performance and percentage, while reducing the processing time. T o
improve the performance of the GWO, a mathematical prediction technique can be used to estimate the next
positions of the wolves.
Sackey et al. suggested a low-ener gy clustering brain stage optimization algorithm (EEC-BSO)
[13]
. A superior BSO method was also used to find the right cluster centers to achieve the coverage percentage, packet
data rate, and ener gy effi ciency . A cluster-transmission-based energy usage model with an accurate WSN was
also explored.
Selvi et al. suggested a clustering approach based on the gravitational strategy and clustered gravitational
routing methodology
[14]
. A Fuzzy-logic-based inferential inference scheme was also developed to identify the
most suitable nodes as cluster head nodes from among the nodes displayed within each cluster . The remaining
energ y availability was communicated effi ciently across the nodes using an intelligent-agent-based method.
Sahoo et al. suggested the PSO technique coupled with the energy-ef ficient clustering and sink mobility
(PSO-ECSM) to address the chosen CH and sink movement issues
[15]
. The PSO-ECSM tool was used to
determine the best values of these variables. By tackling the hybrid method, sink mobility will be explored to
make the network more cost-effi cient while lowering the end-to-end lag.
Sheriba et al. suggested using the BM-BWO with a Fuzzy logic-based HEED protocol (BMBWFLHEED)
[16]
. The boosted mutation-based black widow optimization (BM-BWO) approach was combined with
the HEED process to determine the optimal remaining energ y in the BMBWFL-HEED. The average direction
method was used to improve the mutation process of the black widow optimization (BWO) method. The
Fuzzy-logic system selects the most relevant and optimal cluster heads.
Swami et al. suggested the ideal end-to-end communication with route selection using an energy optimization method
[17]
. Monitoring the data transmission is critical for establishing sensor networks. The suggested
methodology increased the data packet transmission eff ectiveness by 25% while extending the life of the
WSN, and allowing for more effective file transfer for national healthcare monitoring. No secure data transfer
has been identified in the WSNs for national healthcare tracking.
Rajan et al. suggested a summation method for the adaptive neuro-Fuzzy inference systems (ANFIS)
depending on a defect-tolerant WSN neuro-Fuzzy optimization model estimator
[18]
. For the WSNs, this
method suggests employing an adaptive neuro-Fuzzy inference scheme with intra-and intercluster failuredetection estimators. However , the planned NFOM computation for several prospective locations of vital
positions was not provided.
Rawat et al. suggested an energ y-efficient clustering protocol (PSO-EEC) based on the particle swarm
optimization to improve the network life and achievement
[19]
. The proposed protocol employs the particle
swarm optimization strategy to select the network cluster head and relay nodes. The best node for the cluster
head job was chosen using a PSO-based fitness function that considered the node energy ratios and node
Saziya T ABBASSUM, et al. Effective data transmission through energy-ef ficient clustering and Fuzzy-Based IDS routing approach in WSNs 3

degrees. Nonetheless, the use of various levels of heterogeneity and other appropriate criteria was not mentioned to create CH fitness.
Arya et al. suggested a low-ener gy deep belief network (DBN)-based routing procedure that enhances data
transmission via the chosen path
[20]
. Consequently , the packet delivery ratio (PDR) improved. Initially , the
network nodes were clustered using the reinforcement learning (RL) method, which rewards the nodes
belonging to a specific cluster .
Alhayani et al. suggested two-way image transmission to the Corvus Coron module as a built-in interaction
in the CS transmission through a security framework, presenting an energy-ef ficient model
[21]
. The wellknown arbitrary network conjecture in the CS provides excellent entertainment value. The test had a low error
rate and short computational time.
Al-Otaibi et al. suggested a metaheuristic cluster -based routing (HMBCR) method for WSN
[22]
. This
technique starts with a clustering procedure based on the brainstorm optimization with levy distribution (BSOLD), which uses a fitness function that considers four different characteristics: energ y , distance to neighbors,
distance to the base station, and network load. However , this method does not address the power- supply issue
in perpetual and unattended WSNs.
T able 1 lists some related studies and their limitations. According to the literature, countless task-scheduling
systems have been designed to achieve ener gy-efficient and safe data transfer in the WSN. Based on the
abovementioned studies, several significant challenges have arisen in data transmission. In security-based data
transmission
[17]
, NFOM calculation for diff erent potential locations is needed
[18]
, and appropriate parameters to
create the fitness for the CH are not mentioned
[19]
. The proposed method was developed to overcome these
issues and enable energ y-effe ctive and safe information transmission.
3 Pr oposed methodology
WSNs have piqued the interest of academics in a wide range of disciplines over the last decade. Furthermore,
such challenges are significant because of the server resource constraints, such as eff icient energ y , stock
limitations, network security , and network longevity . In the proposed method, energy effi ciency and safety are
considered for generating an ener gy-efficient and secure WSN for data transmission. Initially , the nodes are
placed in a specified location, and data are gathered about their neighbors. Then to minimize the energ y usage
while extending the network′s life in a homogenous sensor network LEACH is used. Subsequently , invaders
were detected using a novel security model. The IDS detection model first performs two types of processes:
isolating the node from legitimate nodes, and detecting suspicious nodes from the presumed nodes. Figure 1
depicts the workflow of the proposed method.
Initially , the nodes are deployed in a random manner within specific ranges; they are then initialized to
collect data about the neighboring nodes that are assigned to the LEACH protocol to minimize energ y
consumption. The operations of LEACH are divided into several rounds, and each round consists of two
T able 1 Related work survey

Virtual Reality & Intelligent Hardware February (2024) Vol. 6 No. 14

phases: the setup phase, in which clustering is performed, and steady-state phase, in which the data are sent to
the base station from all the sensors. Initially , when the clusters are formed in the setup phase, all the sensor
nodes decide to become a cluster head (CH) or not in the current round. That is, N sensor nodes are assumed to
be distributed randomly throughout the environment and routinely monitored. The CH receives data from each
cluster from the CM. Sensing systems are typically iterative, and have the same energ y and capacity to
perceive the environment through data calculation and transmission. An asymmetric radio relation is always
found among the nodes. This refers to the requirement that nodes have uniform energ y to complete information transmission in every manner .
Furthermore, the BS is assembled on the exterior of the system. The transmission energ y of the sensor nodes
may vary based on the distance between the receiving nodes. After minimizing the energy , the IDS is detected.
An intrusion detection process is executed to identify the existence of intruders in the network. The proposed
IDS operates in two steps. In the first step, a Fuzzy inference rule is used to distinguish the nodes from the
legitimate nodes within the WSN. In the second step, a trained artificial neural network is used to distinguish
the malicious nodes from the suspected nodes.
3.1 Low energy adaptive clustering hierarchy
T o meet the constraints imposed by the WSNs, several routing solutions have been proposed with the goal of
increasing energ y effi ciency and network lifetime. Hierarchical protocols, such as the LEACH, are the most
promising as they enable clustering. By dividing the network into many clusters and randomly spinning the
CHs, the LEACH is designed to ensure reasonable energy use while improving the eff ectiveness of the WSNs.
LEACH is a routing system that groups the nodes into distinct clusters using a clustering algorithm. Consequently , each cluster has a CH node that gathers data from its members and submits them to the sink.
The commitments of the LEACH protocol are split into turns, each with two phases: setup and data transfer .
Choosing the CHs, creating clusters, and developing a TDMA schedule are all parts of the setup procedure, as
illustrated in Figure 2 .
(a) Setup stage
The initial step is to identify the CHs and group them into network clusters. Each sensor node votes to become

Saziya T ABBASSUM, et al. Effective data transmission through energy-ef ficient clustering and Fuzzy-Based IDS routing approach in WSNs 5

the cluster leader during the current epoch. T o make this decision, the sensor node selects a random number at
the start of each epoch. The available numbers is in the range 0–1. The sensor node becomes the CH if its value
is less than the threshold function of the current epoch. However , it then returns to being a regular node.
Because of this choice of CHs, ener gy is distributed across all the network nodes

epoch, and C denotes the group of nodes that
are yet to be CH. When the CHs are chosen, an
advertising message is sent to the network nodes,
informing them of their status. Subsequently , based
on the strength of the signal received from the CHs,
each sensor node unites with its related CH. The
collected signal exerts a significant force if the CH
is close to the sensor node. When a device decides to
join a CH, the CH receives a message requesting
that the node be added to the cluster membership. When a cluster is formed, each CH transmits its members to
the TDMA schedule. The nodes can use TDMA scheduling to determine when to send the acquired information to the CH.
(b) Data communication stage
The second stage of the data interaction is significantly longer than the first stage and includes multiple frames.
During this phase, each normal node transfers the gathered information to its prospective CH at a specific time
along the TDMA timeline. T o avoid a clash, information is delivered to the sink after merg ing with the CHs
using the CDMA code.
Figure 3 depicts the LEACH design, which is divided into clusters.
3.2 Intrusion detection system
The intrusion-detection process is utilized to determine whether the network has any intruders. The proposed
intrusion detection (ID) system has two processes: first, a Fuzzy inference rule is utilized to distinguish
between the suspicious and legal nodes inside the WSN, and then a trained neural network is used to separate
CH

Figure 2 Setup stage of LEACH.

Virtual Reality & Intelligent Hardware February (2024) Vol. 6 No. 16

the malicious nodes from the suspicious nodes. Fuzzy logic is a simple rule-based method, which is flexible
and lightweight. It is used when the outcome of a system is diffic ult to predict. Owing to the channel and node
constraints, node behavior in a WSN is highly unpredictable. Some real nodes experience unexpectedly high
levels of energy loss. This issue can be addressed using the Fuzzy logic by carefully creating it and evaluating
the regular node behavior before assigning specific trust levels.
(a) Categorizing nodes behavior using Fuzzy logic
Mamdani Fuzzy inference rules are utilized in the first stage to categorize the nodes based on their network
activity . This is achieved by computing the threshold during communication and assigning trust ratings to each
node. The trust value is a measure of each node′s interaction misconduct. The lower the probability of malice,
the higher is the trust value. The parameters used in this strategy are the hop count, packet transmission rate,
and number of packets delivered. This is because the packet loss is crucial for identifying the malicious nodes
in a DoS. Malware can activate and send a portion of the data packets to an assailant at a predetermined time. A
hacked node can draw the internet traf fic in either case; however , instead of forwarding packets to the next
neighbor , it can discard them. In both the cases, a lar ge number of packets are dropped because of the lack of
next hop or incorrect route discovery . In certain DoS attacks, hostile nodes use bots to flood a server with
request packets, resulting in packet loss owing to resource overload. In some DoS attacks, hostile nodes use
bots to send lar ge-demand packets to computers, resulting in packet losses related to resource overload. The
Fuzzy membership function is then utilized to compare each node′s present parameters to this threshold and
allocate a trust rank. Using this rule as a base, the simple Mamdani rule is used to characterize the nodes as
trusted, mistrusted, or adversary nodes. This dynamic procedure assigns varying levels of trust to various
nodes based on the circumstances
[27]
.
T able 2 lists the rule generation of the proposed
Fuzzy-logic method. The hop count, packet
transmission rate, and number of packets delivered are the three parameters used to generate the
Fuzzy rule. In the output, 0 indicates that the node
is untrusted, and 1 indicates that it is trusted. A
node is considered untrusted if its hop count,
number of packets delivered, and packet transmission rate are low . If these parameters are improved, the node is considered trusted. The Fuzzy
inference rule and membership value are adaptable, and can be changed based on the threshold
value without interfering with the workflow of
the proposed algorithm. The system is adaptable
and lightweight because of the use of Fuzzy logic. Nonetheless, the detachment of trusted and mistrusted
nodes is not ideal because the Fuzzy rule base is a forecasting system. The output of the Fuzzy model is
doubled using a trained neural network, eventually isolating the malevolent nodes from mistrusting nodes.
(b) Artificial neural networks
Artificial neural networks (ANNs) are powerful soft computing tools used for long-term forecasts. Further -
more, for the modular system. ANN have been utilized to discover the various DoS assaults, such as the
Blackhole and Sybil attacks. Critical attributes, such as the packet drops, received requests, and packet
forwards must be carefully examined to identify the suspicious attacks on a node in a dynamic system. A

specialized scheme is required to detect unusual behaviors in a node by contrasting its characteristics with
those previously discovered. In this scenario, supervised learning is used with a feedforward system for
training. The proposed ANN classifier utilizes the node variables in the dataset as inputs, and the output
indicates whether a node is malicious or an attacker . During the deployment of the WSN, the ANN is used to
screen for distrust and adversary nodes shifted from the Fuzzy-rule-based scheme. The aforementioned node
specifications are fed into the trained ANN, and the likelihood of the output is used to determine whether the
node is malevolent. Figure 4 shows the general architecture of an ANN.

The operation is continued until the BP mistake is lowered and the condition
BP < 0.1 satisfied. When the
BP fault is suff iciently lowered to satisfy the situation, the ANN is activated for categorization. Next, the
testing procedure is performed, during which an ANN is utilized to confirm the accuracy of the categorization.
During this period, the training data are categorized and validated using the new data. When the accuracy of
the ANN classification is satisfactory , it can be directly applied to the suggested categorization. In the
proposed approach, 80% of the data were trained, and 20% of the data were tested. The ANN produces an
output based on whether a node is malicious or normal.
Figure 5 shows the overall flowchart of the proposed model. The proposed model minimizes energ y
consumption and prolongs the network life. Moreover , attack nodes are detected using the IDS model. In the
IDS model, Fuzzy logic detects the legal and suspicious nodes further , and the suspicious node is analyzed to
Saziya T ABBASSUM, et al. Effective data transmission through energy-ef ficient clustering and Fuzzy-Based IDS routing approach in WSNs 9

predict its appropriate condition, such as normal or
attack, using an ANN.
4 Results and discussion
The proposed energ y-ef ficient clustering and Fuzzybased IDS routing approach for data transmission in
the WSNs are tested on the MA TLAB software with
the following system configuration: Processor: Intel
(R)Core™ i5-3330s CPU@2.70 GHz, System type:
64-bit operating system, x64 based processor ,
Memory (RAM):8.00 Gb (7.88 Gb usable).
Initially , the sensor nodes are deployed to obtain
the various types of information. The sensed data
are collected at the BS via routing. A LEACH is
proposed for routing in the homogeneous sensor
networks. In the proposed study , a LEACH was used
to minimize energy consumption and prolong network life; it randomly selects the cluster heads of the
nodes and assigns this role to dif ferent nodes according to a round-robin management policy to
ensure fair energy dissipation between nodes. The system nodes are then detected regardless of whether they
are normal or attacked. The intrusion detecting process act in two steps. In the first step, the Fuzzy inference
rule is suspected to initially segregate the nodes from legitimate nodes within the WSN, and a trained neural
network is utilized in the second stage to finally distinguish the malicious nodes from the suspected nodes. The
NSLKDD
[28]
was utilized in the proposed approach to validate its performance.
Dataset description:
The NSL-KDD dataset contains essential records from the complete KDD dataset. In each record, there are 41
attributes unfolding the diff erent features of the flow , and a label is assigned to each as either an attack type or
normal. The 42
nd
attribute contains data on the five classes of network connection vectors, which are categorized as one normal class and four attack classes. The four attack classes are further grouped into DoS,
Probe, R2L and U2R.
An experimental study was conducted using the sensor
nodes deployed in a farmland area of
500 × 500m 2 , 100
nodes were deployed, and each sensor node had an energ y
range of 10 joules. Several simulation parameters were
considered in the analysis, as listed in T able 3 .
Figure 6 shows the node deployment. The nodes were
placed randomly and there was no appropriate pattern for
deployment. All the deployed nodes were static and not in
motion. The deployed nodes were then formed into clusters, as shown in the figure. Furthermore, the BS was
located in the central region. Figure 7 also depicts the CH selection in a specific area. The proposed LEACH
algorithm was used to select the CHs. T o ensure equitable energy dissipation among the nodes, LEACH

randomly selects the CHs and allocates this function to various nodes using a round-robin strategy . After
selecting a CH, each node transmits a message to its particular CH. This information is transmitted to the BS
and stored on the server .
Some performance metrics, such as the average residual energ y , packet delivery ratio, delay , and throughput,
were analyzed for the proposed method by adjusting the size of the nodes used to identify the effectiven ess of
the proposed method. Figure 8a shows the average residual energ y of the proposed technique with varying
node counts. By adding the energy used when the sensor node is in each state, the sensor node can be
determined to be a residential node. The average residual energy of the system increased as the node size
increased. The node sizes were 25, 50, 75, and 100. The corresponding average residual energ ies of these
nodes were 6.5%, 7%, 8.5%, and 9%, respectively . This indicates that the proposed method produced a better
average residual energy value.
Figure 8b shows the packet delivery ratio of the proposed technique with varying node counts. The packet
delivery ratio is the proportion of the data packets sent to the BS created by the sensors at the sources and starts
cognitive channel traffic. The packet delivery ratio of the proposed system decreased as the size of the nodes

increased. The number of nodes was varied as 25,
50, 75, and 100. The packet delivery ratio of the
proposed method using these nodes were 0.85%,
0.77%, 0.70%, and 0.65%, respectively . This
clearly indicates that the proposed method produces a better packet delivery ratio with fewer
nodes.
Figure 9a shows the delay time of the proposed
approach for the varying node counts. The graph
shows the delay time on the X -axis and number of
iterations on the Y -axis. When the number of
nodes increases, the delay time also increases.
When the number of nodes in the proposed
scheme was 25, the proposed system produced a
shorter delay time. The delay of the proposed
system was obtained at diff erent iterations. Figure
9b shows the throughput of the proposed technique for the varying node counts. The graph was
plotted based on the number of iterations on the X -
axis and the throughput in Mbps on the Y -axis. As the number of nodes in the suggested system increased, so
did the throughput. The throughput of the proposed method varied with the number of iterations; it increased
as the number of iterations increased. When the size of the nodes was 100 and number of iterations was 10, the
proposed system produced a high throughput value.
Following the energy-ef ficient clustering analysis, an intrusion detection procedure was performed to detect
the presence of invaders in the system. The Fuzzy interference rule was first used to distinguish between the
nodes and genuine nodes inside the WSN. Subsequently , a trained neural network was utilized to distinguish
between the evil and presumed nodes. The proposed Fuzzy-based intrusion detection system was evaluated
using metrics, such as hop count, number of delivered packets, and packet delivery ratio. Furthermore, the
performance of the proposed intrusion detection and prevention technique was demonstrated by comparing it
with the existing classifiers, such as KNN, NB, and SVM. Performance metrics were used to compare the
proposed and existing classifiers.
Figure 10 shows the comparison of the accuracy metrics of the proposed and existing classifiers. The
proposed method employs an ANN to distinguish the and abnormal nodes. The accuracy of the proposed

method using the ANN classifier was 97%. However , the accuracies of the existing techniques using the SVM, KNN, and NB
classifiers were 90%, 84%, and 80%, respectively . It is clear that
the accuracy of the proposed technique utilizing the ANN classifier is superior to the existing classifiers for classifying normal
and abnormal nodes. The comparison of the error metrics for the
proposed and existing classifiers is shown in Figure 1 1 . The
proposed method employs an ANN to distinguish the normal and
abnormal nodes. The error in the proposed method using the
ANN classifier was 0.03. However , the error values obtained
using the existing techniques, such as SVM, KNN, and NB
classifiers were 0.1, 0.17, and 0.2, respectively . This clearly indicates that the error value of the proposed method using the
ANN classifier was lower than that of the existing classifiers.
Figure 12 shows the contrast between the specificity metrics
for the proposed and existing classifiers. The proposed method
employs an ANN to distinguish the normal and abnormal nodes.
The specificity of the proposed method using the ANN classifier
was 97%. However , the specificities of the existing techniques
using the SVM, KNN, and NB classifiers were 92%, 86%, and
82%, respectively . Clearly , the specificity of the proposed
method using the ANN classifier is superior to that of the existing classifiers. The comparison of the sensitivity metrics of
the proposed and existing classifiers is shown in Figure 13 . The
proposed method employs an ANN to distinguish the normal and
abnormal nodes. The sensitivity of the proposed method using
an ANN classifier was 95%. However , the sensitivity values of
the existing techniques using the SVM, KNN, and NB classifiers
were 89%, 85%, and 79%, respectively . It is clear that the proposed technique using the ANN classifier has a higher sensitivity value than that of the existing classifiers.
Figure 14 presents a comparison of the precision metrics of
the proposed and existing classifiers. The proposed method
employs an ANN to distinguish the normal and abnormal nodes.
The precision of the proposed method using the ANN classifier
was 97%. However , the precision values of the existing techniques using the SVM, KNN, and NB classifiers were 95%,
90%, and 85%, respectively . The precision of the proposed
method using the ANN classifier was clearly higher than that of
existing classifiers. Figure 15 shows an evaluation of the falsepositive rate (FPR) metrics of the proposed and existing classifiers. The proposed method employs an ANN to
distinguish the normal and abnormal nodes. The FPR of the proposed method using the ANN classifier was
0.025. However , the FPR values of the existing techniques using the SVM, KNN, and NB classifiers were
0.05, 0.08, and 0.1, respectively . It is clear that the proposed technique using the ANN classifier has a higher
FPR value than that of the existing classifiers.

Figure 16 presents a comparison of the false negative rate
(FNR) metrics of the proposed and existing classifiers. The
proposed method employs an ANN to distinguish the normal
and abnormal nodes. The FPR of the proposed method using
the ANN classifier was 0.032. However , the FNR values of the
existing techniques using the SVM, KNN, and NB classifiers
were 0.05, 0.09, and 0.1, respectively . It is clear that the FNR
value of the proposed method using the ANN classifier is superior to that of the existing classifiers. Figure 17 shows a
comparison of the F1_Score metrics for the proposed and
current classifiers. The proposed method employs an ANN to
distinguish between the normal and anomalous nodes. The
F1_Score of the proposed technique using the ANN classifier
was 96%. However , the F1_Score values of the existing
techniques using the SVM, KNN, and NB classifiers were
90%, 85%, and 82%, respectively . It is evident that the proposed technique using the ANN classifier has a higher F1
Score than that of the existing classifiers.
Figure 18 presents a comparison of the Kappa values of the
proposed and current classifiers. The proposed method employs an ANN to distinguish normal and abnormal nodes. The
Kappa value of the proposed method using the ANN classifier
was 94%. However , the kappa values of the existing techniques using SVM, KNN, and NB classifiers were 90%, 85%,
and 80%, respectively . The proposed approach employing the
ANN classifier had a higher kappa value than that of the
current classifiers. Based on the overall results, the proposed
intrusion-detection scheme based on the Fuzzy logic and ANN
classifier is best suited for classifying the normal and abnormal nodes in a WSN.
Finally , the performance of the proposed model was
compared to that of the other models, such as C-SSA, Grid
clustering, geo-clustering, F ABC-EACO clustering, and
T aylor C-SSA, as presented in T able 4 . This comparison
shows that the proposed model provides better outcomes,
such as 0.9% effi ciency , 0.4 delays and 0.1% throughput.
This proves that the proposed method is energ y-ef ficient and
secures data transmission by detecting the attack nodes.

sections.name: Conclusion
sections.content:
An energy-ef ficient clustering and intrusion detection system
(IDS) routing approach was developed for data transmission in
a WSN. T o transfer data in an energ y-ef fective manner , the
proposed approach utilizes a low-ener gy adaptive clustering
hierarchy (LEACH); fuzzy logic and ANN classifiers were
used for the IDS. Initially , the nodes were randomly placed in
the network. LEACH then chooses the CHs at random and
allocates this function to various nodes using a round-robin
management program to ensure reasonable energ y loss among
nodes. Finally , intrusion detection procedures are launched to indicate the presence of intruders in the scheme.
W ithin the WSN, Fuzzy interference was utilized to detach the nodes from legitimate nodes, and an ANN was
utilized to separate the malicious nodes from suspicious nodes. The proposed classification model was tested
using several metrics that achieved better performance, such as an accuracy of 97%, precision of 97%, error
value of 0.03, specificity of 97%, sensitivity of 95%, F1_Score of 96%, FNR of 0.032, FPR of 0.025, and
Kappa value of 94%. Thus, the LEACH and Fuzzy-based IDS approaches are the best for transmitting data in
WSNs. In future work, advanced protocols will be used to eff iciently transfer data, and an artificial intelligence
model will be used to detect dead cluster heads to overcome the problems of corrupted and dead network paths.
Declaration of competing interest
W e declare that we have no conflict of interest.
